passage: The new zoning regulations require property developers to include a minimum of fifteen percent affordable housing units in any residential project exceeding twenty units, this requirement was negotiated over two years of contentious public hearings and represents a compromise between developer interests and housing advocacy groups.
question: Which choice best corrects the sentence structure error in the passage?

choices.A: The new zoning regulations require property developers to include a minimum of fifteen percent affordable housing units in any residential project exceeding twenty units—a requirement negotiated over two years of contentious public hearings that represents a compromise between developer interests and housing advocacy groups.
choices.B: The new zoning regulations require property developers to include a minimum of fifteen percent affordable housing units in any residential project exceeding twenty units; this requirement was negotiated over two years of contentious public hearings and represents a compromise.
choices.C: The new zoning regulations require a minimum of fifteen percent affordable housing in residential projects, this requirement was negotiated over two years and represents a compromise between developer interests and housing groups.
choices.D: No change; 'this requirement' begins a clause that may follow a comma when it clarifies a preceding rule.

explanation: The sentence is a comma splice—two independent clauses joined by only a comma. The second independent clause beginning with 'this requirement' needs a semicolon, period, or coordinating conjunction. Choice B uses a semicolon to correctly join the two independent clauses. Choice A converts the second clause into an appositive noun phrase ('a requirement negotiated over two years...'), which is grammatically elegant but makes more significant structural changes. Choice C preserves the comma splice in a shortened version. Choice D incorrectly claims a comma before 'this requirement' is sufficient when it begins an independent clause.
